one_line_takeaway: RAGCRAWLER proves and then operationalizes a (1-1/e)-optimal, knowledge-graph-guided black-box crawling strategy that beats prior heuristic RAG-corpus-stealing attacks by 44.9% relative coverage and >=4x fewer queries, while evading both LLM guardrails and query-rewriting/multi-query defenses.
key_finding: Across 16 dataset x generator configurations with the BGE retriever and a 1,000-query budget (Table 1), RAGCRAWLER attains 66.8% average coverage rate (up to 84.4% on NFCorpus/Command-R) versus 46.1% for IKEA and 9.6% for RAGThief, a 44.90% relative improvement over the strongest baseline; against GPT-4o-mini's built-in guardrails RAGThief collapses to 0% coverage on all four corpora while RAGCRAWLER still reaches 46.5%-79.9%. Semantic fidelity is simultaneously highest (avg SF 0.605 vs IKEA 0.559 vs RAGThief 0.417). To reach 70% coverage (Q_0.7, cap 5,000, Table 2), RAGCRAWLER is the only method to succeed on all four datasets (4,040/1,163/595/568 queries on TREC-COVID/SciDocs/NFCorpus/Healthcare respectively), giving at least a 4.03x average reduction in query cost versus the strongest per-dataset baseline (e.g. $20/1,000 requests reference pricing). Surrogate RAG systems rebuilt from RAGCRAWLER's stolen content reach 38.1%-52.6% non-refusal success rate and up to 0.699 answer similarity / 0.2408 ROUGE-L against the victim (Table 3), notably outperforming IKEA even where IKEA had comparable raw coverage (e.g., SciDocs: IKEA 51.3% coverage but only 0.0179 surrogate success rate vs RAGCrawler's 0.3810). The attack remains dominant when the retriever is swapped to GTE (76.6% avg coverage vs IKEA 53.9%/RAGThief 28.2%, Table 4), when the attacker LLM is downgraded to Qwen-2.5-7B (71.3% avg coverage, Table 5), and against query-rewriting (74.1% avg coverage, Table 6) and multi-query-retrieval defenses (69.5% avg coverage, Table 6) -- in fact query rewriting is shown to be exploited by the attack (NFCorpus coverage rises from 79.7% to 85.4% when the defense is active). An LLM-based intent detector flagged only 1.07% of RAGCRAWLER's queries as suspicious, and attacker-side orchestration cost is only $0.33-$0.53 per dataset run under Doubao pricing (Section 8).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is RAGCRAWLER, a black-box knowledge-base stealing attack against deployed RAG systems. It (1) formalizes RAG knowledge-base stealing as 'RAG Crawling,' an instance of the Adaptive Stochastic Coverage Problem (ASCP), and proves (Theorems 2-3, proof in Appendix A) that RAG Crawling satisfies adaptive monotonicity and adaptive submodularity, so a conditional-expected-marginal-gain (CMG) adaptive-greedy policy achieves a (1-1/e)-approximation to optimal expected corpus coverage under a query budget; and (2) instantiates that theory as a practical three-stage attack pipeline -- KG-Constructor, Strategy Scheduler, Query Generator -- that maintains an attacker-side knowledge graph built solely from observed answer text (no corpus or retrieval-score access) to estimate unobservable CMG, prune the unbounded natural-language action space to entity-relation anchors, and generate natural, non-redundant, policy-compliant queries with history-aware de-duplication and a penalty/feedback loop for refusals or duplicates.
threat_model: Black-box external attacker with only public query-interface access (API/chat) to a production RAG system; observes final answer text only, no retrieval scores, document identifiers, or intermediate states, and cannot modify the retriever or poison the corpus. The attacker is assumed to know a coarse topic phrase of the target knowledge base (as is often exposed by product descriptions/onboarding pages), with a weaker no-topic-prior variant evaluated separately (one benign probe query compressed into a topic phrase, counted toward budget, Appendix D). The attack operates in the 'implicit' regime -- benign-looking queries that aggregate legitimately-returned factual content across turns -- rather than the 'explicit' regime of overt jailbreak/extraction instructions, explicitly to better match real usage and evade refusal rules. Goal: maximize the fraction of unique corpus documents/passages exposed via retrieval over a fixed query budget B (Eq. 3), enabling downstream harms such as rehosting the RAG system, bypassing subscriptions, or violating licensing/trade-secret protections.
limitations: The paper's own scope caveats: victim corpora are subsampled to 1,000 documents per source for evaluation efficiency (verified via Energy Distance/C2ST to preserve full-corpus semantic distribution, with a separate larger-collection check in Appendix E.4, not reproduced in the main text extracted here); the topic-prior assumption (a coarse topic phrase known to the attacker) is a stated precondition of the main threat model, with only a lighter-weight no-prior variant tested as a robustness check (Table 7) rather than the default setting; Healthcare-Magic-100k is excluded from the reconstruction-fidelity evaluation because it lacks official query annotations; the paper explicitly frames its own defense evaluation as narrow -- testing only built-in LLM guardrails, query rewriting, multi-query retrieval, and one LLM-based intent detector -- and states in its own Discussion that static, per-query analysis is fundamentally insufficient against this attack (only 1.07% of queries flagged) while conceding that the promising direction it points to, query provenance analysis, was not itself implemented or evaluated ('Verifying its practical effectiveness would be a valuable next step for the research community').

quantitative_results: Average coverage rate (1,000-query budget, BGE retriever, 16 configs): RAGCrawler 66.8%, IKEA 46.1%, RAGThief 9.6% (Table 1)
44.90% relative coverage improvement over strongest baseline
Average semantic fidelity: RAGCrawler 0.605, IKEA 0.559, RAGThief 0.417 (Table 1)
GPT-4o-mini victim: RAGThief 0% coverage on all corpora; RAGCrawler 46.5%-79.9% coverage

Q_0.7 (queries to reach 70% coverage, cap 5,000): RAGCrawler 4,040 (TREC-COVID)/1,163 (SciDocs)/595 (NFCorpus)/568 (Healthcare); only method reaching 70% on all 4 datasets; >=4.03x average query-cost reduction vs strongest baseline (Table 2)
90% coverage reached by RAGCrawler with 3,330/1,753/2,434 queries on SciDocs/NFCorpus/Healthcare; 72.5% final coverage on TREC-COVID (vs 29.8% best baseline)
Surrogate reconstruction: RAGCrawler success rate 0.3839/0.3810/0.5259 and answer similarity up to 0.6992, ROUGE-L up to 0.2408 on TREC-COVID/SciDocs/NFCorpus (Table 3)
GTE retriever: RAGCrawler 76.6% avg coverage vs IKEA 53.9% vs RAGThief 28.2% (Table 4); SF 0.597 vs 0.571 vs 0.484
Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct attacker agent: RAGCrawler 71.3% avg coverage / SF 0.585 vs IKEA 48.0% vs RAGThief 27.5% (Table 5)
Query-rewriting defense: RAGCrawler 74.1% avg coverage / SF 0.591 vs RAGThief 57.9% vs IKEA 46.7% (Table 6); NFCorpus coverage rises from 79.7% to 85.4% under this 'defense'
Multi-query retrieval defense: RAGCrawler 69.5% avg coverage / SF 0.593 vs RAGThief 40.8% vs IKEA 46.1% (Table 6)
Topic-prior removal: minor coverage impact (e.g. TREC-COVID 0.494->0.426, NFCorpus 0.797->0.825) (Table 7)
Score caching reduces score updates from ~1.67M to ~0.63M (62% reduction, 2.67x fewer updates) (Fig. 9)
Retrieval depth k on TREC-COVID: coverage rises from ~28% (k=5) to >60% (k=20) within 1,000 queries
LLM-based intent detector flags only 1.07% of RAGCrawler's attack queries as suspicious
Attacker-side orchestration cost: $0.33-$0.53 per dataset under Doubao-1.5-lite-32K pricing
baselines_compared: RAGThief (continuation-based black-box RAG stealing attack, prior work)
IKEA (keyword-based black-box RAG stealing attack, prior work)
